label: fiction
title: The Salted Claim
charge: Fraud — selling shares in a mine he had salted
elements: The mine's richness was falsely shown.
The accused created the false showing.
He sold shares on the strength of it.

beats.text: The district's most respected assayer, visibly unhappy to be called: his assays of the sample rock were accurate. 'Rich ore. I stand by my numbers.'
beats.reveal_note: His numbers were honest — that was the design. You don't fool the buyers; you feed true assays of doctored rock and let honesty do the selling.

beats.text: A metallurgist examines gold recovered from the working face: the flakes are impact-flattened, and they carry smears of lead.
beats.reveal_note: Nature does not fire gold into rock. Flattened flakes and gun-lead are the signature of salting: charge a shotgun with dust, and shoot the fortune in.

beats.text: A parade of old diggers for the defence: thirty years on the fields and never a crooked deal. 'If he says there's gold, there's gold.'
beats.reveal_note: Thirty honest years are what made the trick work. Reputation was the one asset the claim genuinely held.

beats.text: The consulting engineer's drill cores, taken at the buyers' insistence: beyond the first six inches of the face, the rock is barren — values die to nothing in every direction.
beats.reveal_note: A real strike goes somewhere. Six inches of wealth wrapped around a mountain of nothing is a stage set, measured.

beats.text: The judge: a true report of a false thing is still a false pretence — the honesty of the assayer does not launder the rock.
beats.reveal_note: The instruction that unpicks the cleverness: he outsourced the lying to honest men.

twist: Every assay was honest; the rock was not. The gold had been fired into the face with a shotgun — flattened flakes, lead smears — and the buyers' own drill found nothing six inches in. He sold them a mountain wearing six inches of make-up.
